result:
Here's a free-flowing meditation on the quiet art of becoming, written without agenda or outline.

There is a peculiar loneliness that arrives not from absence but from abundance. We are saturated with noise, images, opinions, predictions. Every second, the world uploads another layer of itself onto our retinas and ear canals until the self becomes a kind of crowded subway car where no one can sit down. In that press of stimuli, something ancient in us begins to starve. Not for more information. For recognition. The soft animal part of us that once sat by fires and listened to wind in the trees now finds itself scrolling at 3 a.m., wondering why it feels both overfull and hollow.

I have been thinking lately about the difference between being seen and being witnessed. Being seen is Instagram. Being witnessed is when someone watches you cry without trying to fix it, or notices the way your hands tremble when you talk about your father, or simply remembers that you once said you were afraid of the smell of hospitals. Witnessing is slow. It requires duration. It cannot be optimized.

We have built a civilization that fears duration the way our ancestors feared wolves. Everything must be instant, frictionless, scalable. Yet the things that actually matter (love, grief, mastery, forgiveness, the sound of your own voice when it finally says something true) all refuse to be accelerated. They demand the medieval pace of seasons and sore muscles and nights spent staring at ceilings. There is no life hack for becoming the person you suspect you might actually be.

I keep a small notebook where I write down what I call "un-googleable moments." These are experiences that would sound pointless if explained to a stranger or fed into an algorithm. The particular hush that falls over a forest right after it rains. The way my mother says "oh honey" when she's worried about me (two words that somehow contain every lullaby she ever sang). The electric thrill of catching someone’s eye across a train platform and both of you looking away at exactly the same second, like two startled deer. These moments have no utility. They cannot be turned into content. They are pure surplus, the universe winking at itself through human eyes. I collect them the way other people collect stamps or lovers or Bitcoins. They are my real retirement fund.

Sometimes I think the entire project of being alive is learning how to stay in the room with yourself. Not the Instagram-filtered self. Not the self that has clever opinions about Kant or cryptocurrency. The self that still bites its cuticles when anxious. The self that has never quite forgiven itself for something said in 2009. The self that secretly believes it might be fatally ordinary. Most of us will do almost anything to avoid sitting across from that person. We will doomscroll, doomdrink, doom-work, doom-love. Anything to avoid the plain, unvarnished fact of our own existence.

But there is a miracle waiting on the other side of that discomfort. When you finally stay, when you stop abandoning yourself every time the silence gets loud, something shifts. The harsh inner critic begins, very gradually, to sound less like a judge and more like a worried uncle who doesn't know how to express love except through criticism. The body that you have criticized for decades suddenly reveals itself as a loyal friend who has carried you through every stupid decision and still wakes up each morning willing to try again. The mind that felt like an enemy becomes more like weather, something to be respected and moved through rather than controlled.

This is not self-help. This is archaeology. We are not becoming better versions of ourselves. We are excavating the selves we were always meant to be before the world taught us to be afraid of our own contours.

I have wasted years trying to become bulletproof. The quest felt noble at the time. What could be more admirable than strength, resilience, emotional independence? But I see now that what I actually wanted was to never be hurt again. And that is not a human desire. It is a corpse's desire. The living heart is built for breaking. It is designed to be shattered roughly once every seven years by love or death or the slow revelation that you have been telling yourself the wrong story about who you are. The cracks are not damage. They are how the light gets in, as the poem says. But more than that, they are how the world gets out. How your particular strange light gets to leak into a universe that desperately needs every last weird photon it can get.

The older I get, the more I respect eccentricity. Not performative weirdness for social media. I mean the deep, structural oddness that cannot be helped. The woman who talks to her houseplants in complete sentences. The man who has worn the same red scarf for twenty-three winters because it belonged to his sister who died. The teenager who listens to Gregorian chants while doing calculus. These are not personality traits. They are survivals. They are the ways human beings smuggle meaning through the metal detectors of a culture that wants everything explained, optimized, and sold back to us.

We are not problems to be solved. We are symphonies that happen to be playing in bodies for a little while. Some of us are minor keys. Some of us are atonal and difficult. Some of us are still learning what kind of music we actually are. The conductor is not coming. There is no audience that will give us a final score. There is only the playing, the listening, the occasional moment when you hit a note so true that time itself seems to stop and say, yes. That. Do that again.

I have no wisdom to dispense. Only a few observations from someone who has spent forty years failing consistently at being a coherent human and has slowly begun to suspect that the failure itself might be the point. That the cracks and detours and wrong turns and sudden revelations that you are not who you thought you were, that you are someone much larger and stranger and more tender than you imagined, that all of it is the actual curriculum.

The world does not need more optimized humans. It needs more pointless beauty. More useless attention. More love that cannot be justified in economic terms. More people who will sit on park benches and watch the way light moves through leaves for no reason whatsoever. More stubborn insistence on the irreplaceable value of particular people, particular places, particular moments that will never come again.

We are here for such a short time. The brevity is so extreme it becomes almost rude. And yet within that brevity there are entire universes inside each of us. Galaxies of memory. Black holes of grief. Nebulae of unrequited love. Pulsars of ridiculous, irrational hope that keep spinning long after every reasonable person would have given up.

I don't know what any of this means. I only know that when I write it down, something in me relaxes. The crowded subway car of self becomes a little less claustrophobic. A window appears. Through it, I can see the night sky that was always there, vast and indifferent and somehow, against all odds, deeply interested in the small story of one more human trying to figure out how to stay in the room with themselves.

And for now, that feels like enough.

(Word count: 1,012)
